title: Beware Lest Thou Forget the Lord

sections.title: Introduction
sections.bodyText: Moses's earthly ministry began on a mountain, when God spoke to him from a burning bush (see Exodus 3:1–10). It also ended on a mountain, more than 40 years later, when God gave him a glimpse of the promised land from the top of Mount Nebo (see Deuteronomy 34:1–4). Moses had spent his life preparing the children of Israel to enter that promised land, and the book of Deuteronomy records his final instructions, reminders, exhortations, and pleadings with the Israelites. Reading his words makes it clear that the real object of Moses's ministry—the preparation the people needed—wasn't about wilderness survival, conquering enemies, or building a nation. It was about learning to love God, obey Him, and remain loyal to Him. That's the preparation we all need to enter the promised land of eternal life. So although Moses never set foot in the 'land flowing with milk and honey' (Exodus 3:8), because of his faith and faithfulness, he did enter the promised land that God has prepared for all those who follow Him.

scriptureRefs.context: Moses's earthly ministry began on a mountain, when God spoke to him from a burning bush
God gave him a glimpse of the promised land from the top of Mount Nebo
land flowing with milk and honey

sections.title: Love the Lord thy God with all thine heart.
sections.bodyText: The law of Moses included many outward ceremonies and rituals. As you'll see in Moses's counsel in Deuteronomy, the Lord was also concerned about His people's inward state—the spiritual condition of their hearts. In the passages below, look for the word heart, and ponder what it might symbolize. You might think of these passages as a kind of spiritual checkup on your heart. What diagnosis would you give yourself? What treatments would you prescribe to improve the spiritual health of your heart? One way to organize your thoughts could be to draw a heart and write inside it things that the Lord says you should have in your heart. Then you could write outside it things that you should keep out of your heart. How do you show that you love God with all your heart?

look for the word heart — spiritual checkup
look for the word heart — spiritual checkup
look for the word heart — spiritual checkup
look for the word heart — spiritual checkup

conferenceMessages.title: Lovest Thou Me More Than These?
conferenceMessages.speaker: M. Russell Ballard

sections.title: Beware lest thou forget the Lord.
sections.bodyText: Most of the Israelites who would enter the promised land had not seen the plagues in Egypt or crossed the Red Sea. Moses knew that they—and future generations—would need to remember God's miracles and God's laws to remain God's people. What counsel do you find in Deuteronomy 6:4–12, 20–25 that could help you remember the great things God has done for you? What are you inspired to do so that the word of the Lord will daily 'be in thine heart'? (verse 6). You might also study Brother Jan E. Newman's message and consider how you will pass your faith in Christ to future generations.

scriptureRefs.context: counsel to help you remember the great things God has done
word of the Lord will daily be in thine heart
see also reference for remembering the Lord

questions: What counsel do you find in Deuteronomy 6:4–12, 20–25 that could help you remember the great things God has done for you?
What are you inspired to do so that the word of the Lord will daily "be in thine heart"?

conferenceMessages.title: Consider the Goodness and Greatness of God

sections.title: Helping people in need involves generous hands and willing hearts.
sections.bodyText: We haven't yet arrived at the day 'when there shall be no poor among you' (Deuteronomy 15:4), so the principles about helping the poor in Deuteronomy 15 are still valuable, even if the particular practices have changed. Notice what verses 1–15 teach about why we help people in need and what our attitude should be about it. What does it mean to 'open thine hand wide' to people in need? (verses 8, 11). What do you learn from the Lord's example about helping people in need? (see verse 15).

scriptureRefs.context: when there shall be no poor among you
principles about helping the poor in Deuteronomy 15
open thine hand wide to people in need
learn from the Lord's example about helping people in need

What do you learn from the Lord's example about helping people in need?

sections.title: Moses was 'like unto' Jesus Christ.
sections.bodyText: Peter, Nephi, Moroni, and the Savior Himself all commented on the prophecy in Deuteronomy 18:15–19 (see Acts 3:20–23; 1 Nephi 22:20–21; Joseph Smith—History 1:40; 3 Nephi 20:23). What do you learn about the Savior from these verses? Think about what you've read about Moses over the past few weeks. How is Moses 'like unto' Jesus Christ?

scriptureRefs.context: prophecy about a prophet like unto Moses — Jesus Christ
Peter's commentary on the prophecy about Moses
Nephi's commentary on the prophecy about Moses
Joseph Smith History reference to Moses prophecy
Savior's own commentary on the Moses prophecy

questions: What do you learn about the Savior from these verses?
How is Moses "like unto" Jesus Christ?

sections.title: The Lord invites me to choose between good and evil.
sections.bodyText: It might be interesting to compare Moses's words in Deuteronomy 29:9; 30:15–20 with some of Lehi's final teachings to his family in 2 Nephi 2:26–29; 4:4. How did Lehi expand on what Moses taught? What do you find in these passages that inspires you to 'choose life'? (Deuteronomy 30:19).

scriptureRefs.context: Moses's words — compare with Lehi's teachings
Moses's words about choosing good and evil
choose life — Moses's invitation
Lehi's final teachings expanding on Moses
Lehi's final teachings to his family

questions: How did Lehi expand on what Moses taught?
What do you find in these passages that inspires you to "choose life"?

sections.title: I can love God with all my heart.
sections.bodyText: To help your children understand Deuteronomy 6:5, you could draw a heart, an outline of a body, and a strong arm. Your children could point to the pictures as you read the words 'heart,' 'soul,' and 'might' in the verse. How do we show the Lord that we love Him with all our heart, soul, and might?

scriptureRefs.context: love God with all your heart, soul, and might — activity with heart drawing

questions: How do we show the Lord that we love Him with all our heart, soul, and might?

sections.title: I can keep the word of God in my heart.
sections.bodyText: Read with your children the Lord's counsel to the Israelites to put scripture passages where they would see them every day (see Deuteronomy 6:6–9). This might inspire you and your children to think of ways to make sure you don't 'forget the Lord' (verse 12) and to remember what He has done for you. You might point out that Deuteronomy 6:13, 16 and 8:3 helped the Savior during an important moment in His life. To see how, read together Matthew 4:1–10.

scriptureRefs.context: put scripture passages where they would see them every day
don't forget the Lord — verse 12
helped the Savior during an important moment in His life
helped the Savior during His temptation
Savior used these scriptures when tempted by Satan — NEW reference

sections.title: Prophets teach us about Jesus Christ.
sections.bodyText: As you read Deuteronomy 18:18 together, help your children understand that the Prophet like Moses in this verse is Jesus Christ. Maybe you could look at pictures of things Moses did and things Jesus Christ did, and let your children match them (see Gospel Art Book, nos. 15, 16, 38, 39). What has our prophet today taught us about the Savior?

scriptureRefs.context: the Prophet like Moses is Jesus Christ

questions: What has our prophet today taught us about the Savior?

activityRefs.description: Moses pictures matched with Jesus Christ pictures

sections.title: Because of my covenants, I am part of God's people.
sections.bodyText: Talking about Deuteronomy 29:12–13 provides an opportunity for you and your children to talk about covenants they will make or have made with Heavenly Father. What does it mean to be God's people? How do our promises make us God's people? (see verse 13; see also Mosiah 18:8–10). What promises did God and the Israelites make with each other in Deuteronomy 30:8–10? What promises have we made with God? (see Doctrine and Covenants 20:37, 77). How do these promises connect us with Him?

scriptureRefs.context: covenants that make us God's people
promises God and Israelites made with each other
baptismal covenant — what promises have we made with God
promises we have made with God at baptism

What promises have we made with God?

youngWomenTheme.fullText: I am a beloved daughter of heavenly parents, with a divine nature and eternal destiny. As a disciple of Jesus Christ, I strive to become like Him. I seek and act upon personal revelation and minister to others in His holy name. I will stand as a witness of God at all times and in all things and in all places. As I strive to qualify for exaltation, I cherish the gift of repentance and seek to improve each day. With faith, I will strengthen my home and family, make and keep sacred covenants, and receive the ordinances and blessings of the holy temple.

relevantPhrases.phrase: make and keep sacred covenants, and receive the ordinances and blessings of the holy temple
relevantPhrases.connection: Directly parallels the Children section on Deuteronomy 29:12–13 and 30:8–10 — Israel making covenants with God, and our modern covenant equivalents (Mosiah 18, D&C 20)
Mirrors Deuteronomy 6:6–9 — the commandment to teach God's word diligently to children and bind it as a sign in your home

relevantPhrases.phrase: stand as a witness of God at all times and in all things and in all places
relevantPhrases.connection: Echoes Moses's call in Deuteronomy 6:4–12 to remember the Lord continually — not just in the synagogue but in home, daily life, and with future generations

relevantPhrases.phrase: seek and act upon personal revelation and minister to others in His holy name
relevantPhrases.connection: Connects to Moses's role as prophet (Deuteronomy 18:15–19) — receiving and acting on divine communication

aaronicPriesthoodTheme.fullText: I am a beloved son of God, and He has a work for me to do. With all my heart, might, mind, and strength, I will love God, keep His commandments, and use this priesthood to serve others, beginning in my own home. As I strive to serve, exercise faith, repent, and improve each day, I will qualify to receive the Melchizedek Priesthood and make and keep temple covenants. Pressing forward with steadfastness in Christ, I will fulfill my divine destiny.

relevantPhrases.phrase: With all my heart, might, mind, and strength, I will love God
relevantPhrases.connection: DIRECT RESTATEMENT of Deuteronomy 6:5 — 'thou shalt love the LORD thy God with all thine heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy might.' Every young man reciting this theme is quoting Moses. Matthew 22:37–40 (FSY Ch.6 header) is Jesus quoting the same verse.
relevantPhrases.note: The most significant cross-reference in this lesson. Three sources quote the same commandment: Deuteronomy 6:5 (Moses), Matthew 22:37 (Jesus), and the AP Quorum Theme (today's youth). Excellent game question potential.

relevantPhrases.phrase: keep His commandments, and use this priesthood to serve others, beginning in my own home
relevantPhrases.connection: Echoes Deuteronomy 6:6–9 — commandments are to be lived in the home first, then carried outward
Parallels Deuteronomy 29:12–13 — the Israelites making covenants with God, directly referenced in Children section 4

relevantPhrases.phrase: He has a work for me to do
relevantPhrases.connection: Echoes Moses's call — God commissioned Moses at the burning bush (Exodus 3:1–10, lesson intro). Moses was told 'I will send thee' (Exodus 3:10).

annualTheme.text: I Can Do All Things Through Christ Which Strengtheneth Me

relevantPhrases.phrase: I Can Do All Things Through Christ
relevantPhrases.connection: General encouragement theme — connects loosely to Moses's call and the Lord's instruction to 'choose life' in Deuteronomy 30:19. The enabling power of Christ is the lesson's undercurrent.

fsyConnections.title: Love God, love your neighbor
fsyConnections.connectionSummary: This is the highest-relevance FSY chapter for this lesson. Matthew 22:37–40 IS Jesus quoting Deuteronomy 6:4–5 (the Shema). The entire chapter is the New Testament / modern application of Section 1 of this lesson. The AP Quorum Theme, FSY Ch.6, and Deuteronomy 6:5 are all the same commandment across three eras.

relevantPhrases.fsyText: The two greatest of all commandments are to love God and love your neighbor.
relevantPhrases.lessonEcho: Love the Lord thy God with all thine heart — Deuteronomy 6:4–7; look for the word heart in each passage

relevantPhrases.fsyText: Demonstrating love for God: honor the Sabbath, participate in sacrament, fast, pay tithes, use sacred names reverently.
relevantPhrases.lessonEcho: What treatments would you prescribe to improve the spiritual health of your heart?

relevantPhrases.fsyText: Loving God's children begins at home by creating a space where people experience the Savior's presence.
relevantPhrases.lessonEcho: Deuteronomy 6:6–9 — write these commandments on your heart, teach them to your children, bind them in your home

relevantPhrases.fsyText: All people are your brothers and sisters—including people who are different from you or disagree with you.
relevantPhrases.lessonEcho: Deuteronomy 15:1–15 — open thine hand wide to people in need; helping the poor with willing hearts

keyTeachings: God loves you. His perfect love can inspire you to love Him.
The two greatest commandments underlie all scriptural teaching and prophetic counsel.
Loving God's children begins at home.
Loving difficult people: The Lord expects you to love your enemies and pray for those who mistreat you.
questions: How can you feel God's love for you?
How do you love difficult people?
What do you do when home isn't a loving environment?

fsyConnections.title: Ordinances and covenants give you access to God's blessings
fsyConnections.connectionSummary: Directly parallels Children section 4 (Deuteronomy 29:12–13 / 30:8–10 on covenants). The chapter explains baptismal and sacrament covenants — the modern equivalent of Israel's covenant-making at the plains of Moab. Connects to Mosiah 18:8–10 and D&C 20:37, 77 already in the lesson database.

relevantPhrases.fsyText: Covenants function as mutual agreements between individuals and God.
relevantPhrases.lessonEcho: Deuteronomy 29:12–13 — ye stand this day all of you before the LORD your God…that thou shouldest enter into covenant

relevantPhrases.fsyText: When baptized, members promise to take upon yourself the name of Jesus Christ, serve Him, and keep His commandments.
relevantPhrases.lessonEcho: Mosiah 18:8–10 (baptismal covenant) — already in lesson database as a NEW children-section reference

relevantPhrases.fsyText: Power to overcome temptation and trials, forgiveness and cleansing, constant companionship of the Holy Ghost.
relevantPhrases.lessonEcho: Deuteronomy 30:6–10 — the Lord will circumcise thine heart… thou shalt return and obey the voice of the LORD

keyTeachings: Ordinances are sacred ceremonies with symbolic meanings that teach about Jesus Christ.
Covenants deepen trust and enable access to greater spiritual power.
Weekly sacrament participation allows covenant renewal.
questions: Are ordinances necessary for following Christ?
What if I haven't kept my covenants perfectly?

fsyConnections.title: God wants to communicate with you
fsyConnections.connectionSummary: Medium relevance. Connects to Section 2 ('Beware lest thou forget the Lord') — Deuteronomy 6:4–12 commands keeping God's word in the heart so you hear His voice. The FSY chapter on receiving revelation through mind and heart echoes Moses's call to bind God's word on their hearts.

relevantPhrases.fsyText: The Spirit speaks to your mind through inspired thoughts, warnings, clear understanding, and spiritual memories — and to your heart through feelings like comfort, peace, and joy.
relevantPhrases.lessonEcho: Deuteronomy 6:6 — these words shall be in thine heart

keyTeachings: The Spirit leads you to Heavenly Father and Jesus Christ.
Pray and listen for answers that arrive as peace or mental impressions.
Act on spiritual impressions to serve others.
questions: Why do some people have more dramatic spiritual experiences than others?

fsyConnections.title: Walk in God's light
fsyConnections.connectionSummary: Lower relevance. Loosely connects to Section 5 (choosing between good and evil, Deuteronomy 30:19 — choose life). 'Walk in God's light' parallels Moses's invitation to 'choose life.'
